title: Thermal/Infrared Testing: Fundamentals and Equipment
narrationScript: Every object above absolute zero radiates infrared energy, and thermal infrared testing turns that radiation into inspection data. This lesson builds the physics first. The Stefan-Boltzmann law says total radiated power climbs with the fourth power of absolute temperature; Wien's law says hotter surfaces radiate at shorter wavelengths, which puts room-temperature targets near ten micrometers, squarely in the long-wave camera band. Emissivity is the gatekeeper: a real surface emits only a fraction of blackbody radiation, and what it does not emit, it reflects. Polished aluminum at emissivity zero point zero five is nearly a mirror — set the instrument wrong and you measure the surroundings, not the part. From there the equipment ladder runs from contact to noncontact: temperature-indicating crayons and labels, thermocouples and resistance temperature detectors, heat flux gauges, spot pyrometers with their distance-to-spot ratio, ratio pyrometers that cancel emissivity, line scanners that map moving product, and focal-plane-array thermal imagers that deliver a full thermogram at video rates. Choose passive or active heating to match the problem, and the heat pattern becomes a picture of hidden condition.

sections.heading: Heat as a Signal: What Infrared Testing Measures
sections.body:
Thermal/infrared testing (IR) reads the one signal every object broadcasts continuously: **infrared radiation**, electromagnetic energy lying between visible light and microwaves, from about **0.75 μm to 1000 μm** in wavelength. Anything warmer than absolute zero emits it, and hotter surfaces emit more — so an instrument that measures infrared radiation from a distance is measuring **temperature without touching the part**.

The method's logic has two halves:

- **Radiation carries the signal out.** Of the three heat-transfer mechanisms — **conduction** (diffusion through solids and stationary fluids), **convection** (heat carried by moving fluid), and **radiation** (electromagnetic emission that needs no medium at all) — radiation is the only one an IR instrument actually measures.
- **Conduction writes the pattern.** Heat flowing through the part must detour around anything that changes the thermal path: a disbond, an air void, water trapped in honeycomb core, wall thinning, a high-resistance electrical joint. The detour appears at the surface as a warm or cool pattern, and that pattern — not the absolute temperature — is usually the inspection result.

Practical instruments view the two **atmospheric windows** where air transmits infrared freely: **midwave (MWIR, 3–5 μm)** and **longwave (LWIR, 8–14 μm)**. Room-temperature work lives in the longwave band, as the next section's physics will show.

The method's character follows directly: **noncontact, full-field, and fast** — an imager maps thousands of temperatures per frame at safe standoff — but strictly **line of sight**, directly sensitive only to the **surface**, with everything subsurface inferred through heat flow. In an FAA Part 145 shop you will meet both faces of it: a flash lamp pulsing heat across a bonded composite repair, and a camera sweep of a just-landed aircraft hunting cold-soaked water in a rudder core.

sections.heading: The Radiation Physics: Three Laws and Emissivity
sections.body:
A **blackbody** is the ideal radiator — it absorbs every photon that strikes it and emits the theoretical maximum for its temperature. Three laws describe it, and every IR instrument is built on them:

- **Planck's law** gives the full spectral curve: how much energy a blackbody radiates at each wavelength for a given temperature. The other two laws are its summary statistics.
- **The Stefan-Boltzmann law** totals the curve: radiated power per unit area is **M = εσT⁴**, with σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴) and T in **absolute** units. The fourth power is the method's leverage — double the absolute temperature and radiated energy multiplies **sixteenfold** — and it is why a few degrees of difference stands out clearly to a sensitive detector.
- **Wien's displacement law** locates the curve's peak: **λmax ≈ 2898 μm·K ÷ T**. A 300 K room-temperature surface peaks near **9.7 μm**, which is why LWIR cameras own ambient-temperature work; an 800 K engine component peaks near 3.6 μm, MWIR territory; the 5800 K sun peaks at 0.5 μm, in visible light.

Real surfaces fall short of the ideal. **Emissivity (ε)** is the ratio of what a surface actually emits to what a blackbody at the same temperature would emit — 1.0 for the ideal, less for everything real. By **Kirchhoff's law**, a good absorber is an equally good emitter, and for any surface the incident-energy fractions balance: **ε + ρ + τ = 1** (emitted, reflected, transmitted). For an opaque part τ = 0, so **ρ = 1 − ε**: whatever a surface does not emit, it reflects.

Working values tell the story: flat black paint ≈ 0.95, PVC electrical tape ≈ 0.95, human skin ≈ 0.98, heavily oxidized steel ≈ 0.85 — and **bare polished aluminum 0.03–0.1**. That last number is the trap that runs through all of thermography: a shiny metal surface emits almost nothing and mirrors almost everything around it.

sections.caption: An IR instrument receives three contributions from every surface — emitted (ε), reflected (ρ), and transmitted (τ), summing to 1. Two blocks at the same 100 °C tell the story: flat black paint reads true, while polished aluminum (ε ≈ 0.05) mostly mirrors the 25 °C hangar and reads near 30 °C.

sections.heading: Temperature Measurement Principles: Contact and Radiometric
sections.body:
Temperature expresses the mean kinetic energy of a material's molecules; instruments reach it by two distinct routes.

**Contact measurement** brings a sensor into **thermal equilibrium** with the object by conduction — strictly speaking, every contact device reports its own temperature and trusts that it matches the part. Three disciplines follow:

- **Intimate contact.** An air gap insulates; thermal paste, clamping, or welding the junction closes it.
- **Time.** Every sensor has a **response time constant**; read it before equilibrium and you read the history, not the part.
- **Loading.** The sensor's own mass and its leads conduct heat away. On a thin composite skin or a small electronic component, a heavy probe measurably changes the very temperature it reports.

**Noncontact (radiometric) measurement** captures radiation and computes temperature from the blackbody laws. For the displayed number to be true, the instrument must be told — or compensated for — four things:

- **Emissivity** of the surface, set from tables or measured in place (ASTM E1933 gives the practice: compare against a contact reading or a high-ε reference patch).
- **Reflected apparent temperature** — the surroundings the surface mirrors, measured and entered per ASTM E1862; on low-ε surfaces this term can dominate the reading.
- **Atmosphere and distance** — long paths, humidity, and combustion gases attenuate the signal.
- **Spot size** — the target must fill the measurement spot, or the reading averages in background.

One unit discipline binds both routes: the radiation laws use **absolute temperature** — kelvin (°C + 273.15) or rankine (°F + 459.67). Instruments display celsius or fahrenheit, but every radiometric computation underneath runs in absolute units.

sections.title: Before you trust a radiometric reading
sections.body: **Set the emissivity** for the actual surface — paint, oxide, and bare metal on the same part can differ by a factor of ten. **Measure and enter the reflected apparent temperature**, especially indoors near lamps, heaters, and people, or outdoors under the sun. **Fix low-ε surfaces** by adding a high-emissivity reference — PVC electrical tape or a spot of flat paint (ε ≈ 0.95) — and measuring that. **Fill the spot**: a 12:1 distance-to-spot thermometer at 600 mm averages a 50 mm circle, and a smaller target blends into whatever is behind it. Skip any of the four and the instrument will confidently report a number that is not the part's temperature.

sections.heading: Proper Selection of IR Technique
sections.body:
The first question of technique selection: **does the target produce or move heat on its own?**

- **Passive thermography** observes temperature differences the part generates in service: a high-resistance terminal heating under load, a dragging brake, a bearing running hot, heat exchangers, furnace walls. It also exploits **natural transients** — an aircraft descends from altitude cold-soaked, and water trapped in honeycomb core, with its large thermal capacitance, lags the skin's warm-up and prints as a cool patch in a post-flight scan.
- **Active thermography** supplies the heat deliberately, because a part in thermal equilibrium shows no pattern at all. **Flash (pulsed) thermography** fires a millisecond xenon pulse at the surface and images the cooldown: heat trapped above a disbond or delamination makes a warm spot, appearing earlier for shallower defects — echo time grows with depth squared divided by the material's **thermal diffusivity**. ASTM E2582 standardizes the practice for aerospace composite panels and repair patches. **Step heating** soaks the part for seconds when defects lie deeper, and **lock-in thermography** modulates the heat sinusoidally and computes a **phase image**, largely immune to uneven heating and surface emissivity variation, with depth selected by modulation frequency.

With the heating decided, match the instrument to the job:

- **Temperature range and required sensitivity** — resolving a 0.1 °C pattern needs a low-NETD imager, not a dial thermometer.
- **Spectral band** — LWIR for ambient targets and outdoor work; MWIR for hot, fast, or high-sensitivity work.
- **Spatial resolution** — the instantaneous field of view or D:S ratio against target size and standoff distance.
- **Speed** — a flash transient on thin composite skin is gone in fractions of a second; uncooled cameras at 30–60 Hz catch it on thin laminates, while cooled cameras running to kilohertz rates own the fastest events.
- **Environment and access** — wind strips surface heat, solar reflections masquerade as hot spots on low-ε metal, and line of sight must exist at a workable angle.

sections.heading: Contact Equipment: Indicators, Sensors, and Heat Flux Gauges
sections.body:
The contact side of the equipment spectrum trades coverage for simplicity and price:

- **Contact temperature indicators.** Temperature-indicating **crayons, lacquers, and pellets** melt sharply at a rated temperature (accuracy on the order of ±1%) — the classic check that a weldment has reached preheat or stayed under interpass limits. **Irreversible labels** carry a row of windows that blacken permanently as their ratings are passed, leaving a record of the maximum reached. **Cholesteric liquid crystals**, applied as sheets or sprays, play through colors reversibly over a band a few degrees wide — a low-cost, full-field map once used for bond inspection, demanding contact and careful temperature control.
- **Liquid-in-glass and bimetallic dial thermometers** remain the plant's simple, robust point readers.
- **Thermocouples** exploit the **Seebeck effect**: two dissimilar metal wires joined at a measuring junction generate a small EMF that depends on the temperature difference between that junction and the reference junction — about **41 μV/°C for Type K** (chromel-alumel), the general-purpose workhorse. Rugged, tiny, inexpensive, and wide-ranging, they are the default for logging discrete points.
- **Resistance temperature detectors (RTDs)** read the predictable rise of a platinum element's resistance — a **Pt100** measures 100.0 Ω at 0 °C and climbs about 0.39 Ω per °C. More accurate and stable than thermocouples, but slower, more fragile, and carrying a self-heating error from their excitation current.
- **Thermistors** are semiconductor beads whose resistance falls steeply (typically) as temperature rises — extremely sensitive over narrow spans, and nonlinear.
- **Heat flux indicators** measure something different: not temperature but **heat flow rate per unit area (W/m²)**. A differential thermopile spans a thin layer of known thermal resistance; the temperature drop across it is proportional to the flux through it. Bonded to a furnace wall, an insulated surface, or a curing repair, they quantify heat transfer directly — the quantity the temperature devices only imply.

sections.caption: The equipment spectrum from contact to noncontact: indicators answer a threshold at a spot, sensors log a point, pyrometers read radiance at a distance, line scanners map moving product, and focal-plane-array imagers deliver a full radiometric thermogram every frame.

sections.heading: Noncontact Equipment: Pyrometers, Line Scanners, and Thermal Imagers
sections.body:
- **Spot pyrometers (infrared thermometers)** convert single-band radiance to temperature for one spot defined by the **distance-to-spot (D:S) ratio** — a 12:1 unit views a 25 mm circle at 300 mm. The laser dot marks aim, not the measured area, and the emissivity setting governs the answer.
- **Optical (disappearing-filament) pyrometers** are the historic instrument: the operator matches the brightness of a calibrated lamp filament against the glowing target at 0.65 μm until the filament vanishes. They need incandescence — practical above roughly 700 °C — and put a human eye in the loop.
- **Total radiation pyrometers** focus broadband radiation onto a thermopile — rugged, fixed monitors for furnaces and process lines.
- **Ratio (two-color) pyrometers** compute temperature from the **ratio** of radiance in two closely spaced bands. For a gray body — emissivity equal in both bands — ε cancels out of the ratio, and partial blockage by smoke, dust, or a dirty window attenuates both bands together, leaving the reading intact. They mismeasure only when emissivity differs between the two bands.
- **Line scanners** sweep a single detector (or small array) across a line at up to several kilohertz using a **rotating mirror**; the second image axis comes free from **product motion**. Paper webs, steel strip, and rotary kilns get continuous full-width thermal maps from a one-dimensional instrument.
- **Thermal imagers** are the method's flagship: a **focal plane array (FPA)** of thousands to millions of detector elements behind **germanium optics**, since ordinary glass is opaque across the LWIR band. Two detector families divide the work. **Uncooled microbolometers** (vanadium oxide or amorphous silicon) sense the minute heating of each pixel — LWIR 8–14 μm, thermal sensitivity (**NETD**, noise-equivalent temperature difference) around 30–60 mK, frame rates of 9–60 Hz — the maintenance and field workhorse. **Cooled photon detectors** (indium antimonide in the MWIR, mercury cadmium telluride) count photons directly from a cryocooled focal plane near 77 K: NETD near 20 mK and kilohertz windowed frame rates, the choice for fast transients and laboratory active thermography. Either way the product is the **thermogram** — a calibrated map of apparent surface temperature, and the raw material the next lesson's applications and interpretation work is built on.

sections.caption: The IR equipment ladder, contact to noncontact: what each device measures and where it earns its place.
rows: Temperature-indicating crayons, paints, labels
Contact
Sharp melt or irreversible color change at a rated temperature
Go/no-go thresholds: weld preheat and interpass checks, overtemperature witness
Cholesteric liquid crystals
Contact
Reversible color play over a band a few degrees wide
Low-cost qualitative full-field mapping of small areas
Thermocouple (Type K)
Contact
Seebeck EMF at a junction of dissimilar metals, ≈41 μV/°C
Rugged, wide-range point measurement and logging
RTD (Pt100)
Contact
Platinum resistance rises predictably from 100 Ω at 0 °C
Stable, accurate process and laboratory points
Heat flux indicator
Contact
Differential thermopile across a thin barrier reads W/m²
Measures heat flow rate, not temperature: insulation, furnace walls, cure monitoring
Spot pyrometer (IR thermometer)
Noncontact
Single-band radiance with set emissivity; D:S defines the spot
Fast point checks where contact is unsafe or impractical
Ratio (two-color) pyrometer
Noncontact
Radiance ratio of two bands cancels gray-body emissivity
Hot, small, or partially obscured targets: furnaces, molten metal
Line scanner
Noncontact
Rotating mirror sweeps one detector; product motion adds the second axis
Continuous maps of moving webs, strip, and rotary kilns
Thermal imager (FPA)
Noncontact
Microbolometer (uncooled, LWIR) or cooled photon array (MWIR)
Full-field thermograms for passive surveys and active NDT

figures.title: IR equipment spectrum: contact to thermal imaging

checkQuestions.question: A component heats from 300 K to 600 K — its absolute temperature doubles. According to the Stefan-Boltzmann law, the total infrared energy it radiates per unit area:
options: Doubles, since radiated power is proportional to temperature
Increases about fourfold
Increases about eightfold
Increases about sixteenfold, because radiated power scales with the fourth power of absolute temperature
checkQuestions.explanation: M = εσT⁴: doubling the absolute temperature multiplies radiated power by 2⁴ = 16. The law runs on absolute temperature — kelvin, not celsius — which is why every radiometric instrument computes in absolute units even while displaying °C.

checkQuestions.question: An LWIR camera set to ε = 0.95 views a polished aluminum hydraulic fitting that is actually at 90 °C in a 25 °C hangar. The most likely reading is:
options: Slightly above 90 °C, because metals radiate more strongly than nonmetals
Accurate, because the emissivity setting does not affect radiometric readings
Far below 90 °C — near 30 °C — because the low-emissivity surface emits little and mostly reflects the 25 °C surroundings
Exactly 25 °C, because polished metal cannot emit infrared radiation at all
checkQuestions.explanation: Bare polished aluminum has ε ≈ 0.03–0.1, and for an opaque surface ρ = 1 − ε, so over 90% of what the camera receives is reflected hangar radiation at 25 °C. The fix is a high-emissivity patch — electrical tape or flat paint at ε ≈ 0.95 — measured with the emissivity and reflected apparent temperature set correctly. The metal does emit a little; it just emits far less than the setting assumes.

checkQuestions.question: A ratio (two-color) pyrometer is preferred over a single-band radiation pyrometer when:
options: The target's emissivity is uncertain or the sight path is partially obscured, because forming the ratio of two wavelengths cancels gray-body emissivity and common attenuation
The target is below room temperature, where single-band devices cannot respond
A full two-dimensional thermogram of the surface is required
The measurement must be made in contact with the part for the record
checkQuestions.explanation: Temperature comes from the ratio of radiance in two closely spaced bands: a gray body's emissivity appears in both numerator and denominator and cancels, and smoke, dust, or a dirty window attenuates both bands together. Residual error appears only when emissivity differs between the bands. It remains a point instrument — full-field imaging needs a focal-plane-array camera.

checkQuestions.question: Which statement correctly pairs the contact device with its operating principle?
options: Thermocouple — the resistance of a platinum element rises predictably with temperature
A thermocouple generates a small temperature-dependent EMF at the junction of two dissimilar metals (the Seebeck effect), while an RTD reads the predictable resistance rise of a platinum element
RTD — a semiconductor bead whose resistance falls steeply as temperature rises
Heat flux indicator — a crayon that melts sharply at its rated surface temperature
checkQuestions.explanation: The Seebeck EMF (about 41 μV/°C for Type K) is the thermocouple's signal; the Pt100 RTD reads 100 Ω at 0 °C and climbs about 0.39 Ω per °C. The steeply falling semiconductor bead describes a thermistor, and a heat flux indicator measures heat flow rate per unit area (W/m²) with a differential thermopile — not temperature at all.

checkQuestions.question: A composite rudder panel sitting at uniform hangar temperature must be screened for near-surface disbonds. The appropriate IR approach is:
options: Passive imaging, since disbonds radiate strongly on their own
A spot pyrometer survey, since point readings resolve subsurface flaws
Active thermography — apply a controlled heat pulse and image the cooldown, where heat trapped above a disbond appears as a warm spot
A grid of temperature-indicating crayons, which change color over each disbond
checkQuestions.explanation: A part in thermal equilibrium presents no pattern, so passive observation shows nothing — the heat must be supplied. Flash thermography per ASTM E2582 pulses the surface and images the cooldown: a disbond interrupts heat flow into the depth, the surface above it stays warm, and shallower defects appear earlier. Point readings and threshold crayons cannot map a transient subsurface pattern.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-105-2024, Basic Examination Topical Outline §2.8 (IR fundamentals: principles/theory, temperature measurement principles, proper selection of IR technique; equipment from heat flux indicators and contact temperature devices to noncontact pyrometers, line scanners, and thermal imaging)
ASTM E1316, Standard Terminology for Nondestructive Examinations — infrared/thermal examination terms (thermogram, emissivity, infrared radiometer)

ASTM E1933, Standard Practice for Measuring and Compensating for Emissivity Using Infrared Imaging Radiometers, and ASTM E1862, Standard Practice for Measuring and Compensating for Reflected Temperature Using Infrared Imaging Radiometers
ASTM E2582, Standard Practice for Infrared Flash Thermography of Composite Panels and Repair Patches Used in Aerospace Applications
